question_text: Copper has an atomic mass of 64. If one copper atom has a mass of 1.06 × 10⁻²² grams, calculate how many copper atoms are in a pure copper wire with a total mass of 6.4 grams. Show your working.
correct_answer: 6.0 × 10²² atoms (or 60 sextillion atoms). Working: Number of atoms = Total mass ÷ Mass of one atom = 6.4 g ÷ (1.06 × 10⁻²² g) = 6.04 × 10²² atoms ≈ 6.0 × 10²² atoms
explanation: This calculation requires students to apply division with very small numbers in scientific notation, bridging the gap between the macroscopic mass they can measure and the huge number of atoms present. It demonstrates why we need the mole concept in chemistry by showing how many particles make up ordinary amounts of matter.
